title: Variable update
question: What is the final value of x after this code runs?
successMessage: Correct. The second line adds 3 to the old value of x and stores the result back.
failureMessage: Not quite. Python first reads the old value of x, adds 3, and then stores the new result.

title: Expression result
question: What is the value of total after these lines run?
successMessage: Correct. Python evaluates the expression coins + bonus and stores 6 in total.
failureMessage: Remember that total gets the result of the expression coins + bonus, not the two values next to each other.

title: Reassigning a value
question: What is the final value of score?
successMessage: Correct. The second line subtracts 3 from the current value and stores the new value back in score.
failureMessage: The second line changes score. Start from 10, subtract 3, and then keep the new result.

title: Equality operator
question: Which operator checks whether two values are equal in Python?
successMessage: Yes. Double equals compares values, while a single equals sign assigns a value.
failureMessage: Remember: = assigns a value, but == compares whether two values are equal.

title: Will the block run?
question: If lives = 3, will the code inside this if-statement run?
options: No, because 3 is not equal to 1
No, because if only works with strings
successMessage: Correct. The condition lives > 1 is true when lives is 3, so the indented block runs.
failureMessage: Check the comparison carefully: 3 really is greater than 1, so the block runs.

title: Which branch runs?
question: What does this program print?
successMessage: Correct. Because coins is not greater than 0, Python skips the if-block and runs the else-block.
failureMessage: coins > 0 is false when coins is 0, so Python runs the else-block instead.

title: Using print()
question: What does this program print?
successMessage: Exactly. print(name) calls the print function with the value stored in name.
failureMessage: The variable name itself is not printed here. Python prints the value stored in the variable.

title: Function call line
question: Which line is a function call?
successMessage: Correct. print(coins) calls the print function and gives it the value to use.
failureMessage: A function call uses a name followed by parentheses, like print(...).

title: Calling the game helper
question: In this game, what does drop_ball() represent in Python?
successMessage: Correct. drop_ball() is written like a normal Python function call.
failureMessage: Look at the parentheses. In Python, a name followed by parentheses is a function call.

title: Repetition count
question: How many times does the loop body run?
successMessage: Correct. range(3) repeats the loop body three times.
failureMessage: The number inside range(...) tells Python how many times to run the loop body here.

title: Picking a range value
question: Which number should replace ? so the loop runs 5 times?
successMessage: Correct. range(5) runs the loop body five times.
failureMessage: For this beginner loop form, the body runs as many times as the number inside range(...).

title: Loop body indentation
question: Which line belongs to the loop body?
successMessage: Correct. The indented line is inside the loop body.
failureMessage: Python uses indentation to mark the loop body. Only the indented line belongs to the loop.
